form_title: Solidarity Fund Application (Please see reverse side for information and instructions)  Name: _______________________________ Date of Request: _________________________  Address: _____________________________ Home Phone #: _________________________  School/Dept. __________________________ School Phone #: ________________________  Position: ______________________________  Amount Requested: $ ___________ (New: loans up to $1,000 can be granted.)  MTI has taken immediate action to deploy union resources to provide financial support to members impacted by the pandemic. Commencing immediately, the MTI Solidarity Fund is available to provide no interest loans of up to $1,000 for MTI members who suffer a financial impact due to the pandemic.  MTI’s Solidarity Fund is made up of donated contributions from Union members to assist Union brothers and sisters in need. No interest is charged. To assure that the Union has funds to assist other MTI members in need, it is required that one receiving a loan from the MTI Solidarity Fund sign an agreement to repay, via payroll deduction, the borrowed amount in equal deductions over thirty (30) months or less. Deductions will begin six (6) months after receipt of the loan.  1. Coronavirus Pandemic: (please describe the specific extenuating circumstances which have contributed to your current financial need. For example, “Unable to pay bills” is not sufficient information to request a loan, without a specific extenuating circumstance such as, “Unable to pay bills due to spouse being recently laid off from job.”) _____________________________________________________________________________________ _____________________________________________________________________________________ 2. Other reason for request: (please describe the specific extenuating circumstances which have contributed to your current financial need. For example, “Unable to pay bills” is not sufficient information to request a loan, without a specific extenuating circumstance such as, “Unable to pay bills due to spouse being recently laid off from job.”) _____________________________________________________________________________________ _____________________________________________________________________________________  Your signed Solidarity Fund Loan Repayment form must accompany your loan application.  Signature _____________________________ Date ________________________  For Solidarity Fund Committee Use Only Request Approved: ______ Request Denied: ______ MTI Member? ______ Outstanding Balance? Y / N Request Approved for $ ______________
instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.employer: Eastside Middle School – Special Education Department, Madison Metropolitan School District
persona.education: B.S. in Special Education, University of Wisconsin–Madison (2012); M.Ed. in Exceptional Education, University of Wisconsin–Whitewater (2017); Wisconsin DPI licensure in Cross-Categorical Special Education, grades 1–8.
family_members: Renee Thompson (35, partner, line cook at Lakeside Grill)
Mateo Alvarez-Thompson (9, son, 4th-grade student with asthma)
Lucia Alvarez-Thompson (4, daughter, preschooler with mild developmental delays)
Carmen Alvarez (63, mother, retired home health aide, lives in Milwaukee, WI)
Daniel Alvarez (38, older brother, electrician, lives in Kenosha, WI)
Elena Alvarez (34, younger sister, nursing student, lives in Milwaukee, WI)
persona.background_context:
This is Jordan M. Alvarez, a 36-year-old special education teacher living on Madison’s southwest side with their partner, Renee, and their two kids, Mateo and Lucia. Their split-level rental on Maple Crest Drive is modest but cozy—hand-me-down furniture, kids’ art taped to the fridge, and a small backyard where the kids sled on the slightest hint of snow. Weekdays start early: Jordan is usually up by 5:45 a.m., packing lunches, checking their lesson plans, and making sure Mateo has his inhaler in his backpack. Renee often comes home late from closing shifts at Lakeside Grill, so mornings are Jordan’s domain—getting Lucia dressed for preschool, coaxing Mateo through breakfast, and shuttling everyone out the door in the family’s aging 2011 Honda CR-V.

At Eastside Middle School, Jordan works in the Special Education Department with 6th–8th graders who have a mix of learning disabilities, autism spectrum diagnoses, and emotional-behavioral needs. They split their day between co-teaching in inclusive classrooms and running a resource room where students get extra support with reading, math, and executive functioning. The job is demanding—IEP meetings, constant communication with families, and the emotional weight of supporting kids who’ve been through a lot—but Jordan genuinely cares about their students and finds small victories deeply rewarding. They’ve been at Eastside for seven years, long enough to see some of their former students move on to high school, and they’re known among colleagues for being calm, organized, and the one who always remembers which student needs noise-canceling headphones before a fire drill. After school, Jordan often stays an extra hour to provide tutoring for a bit of additional income and to help students who fell behind during the pandemic.

Home life is a constant balancing act. The financial aftershocks of COVID-19 still shape many of Jordan’s day-to-day decisions. Renee’s hours as a line cook never fully returned to what they were before 2020, so their income is a patchwork of Jordan’s steady but modest teacher salary and Renee’s fluctuating paychecks, which can dip when business is slow or when shifts get cut. They’re perpetually about a paycheck behind: one month catching up on utilities, the next trying to get rent current after a winter heating bill spike. In late 2025, the CR-V needed new brakes and tires, which they had to put on a high-interest store credit card just to keep both adults able to get to work. That extra monthly payment has been a strain, especially with rent creeping up and SNAP benefits trimmed back.

Health has been another ongoing thread in their lives. Mateo’s asthma requires careful attention—refills for his inhaler, occasional urgent-care visits when a cold flares up his breathing, and a watchful eye whenever he’s running around outside in cold weather. Since having COVID, his flare-ups seem a bit more frequent, and the medication costs have inched up. Lucia, their four-year-old, was recently diagnosed with mild developmental delays, and she now attends weekly occupational therapy sessions across town. Insurance covers part of it, but the copays and gas add up. Still, Jordan and Renee are grateful that Lucia’s therapists are kind and that she’s already showing progress with fine motor skills and communication. Most Tuesday afternoons, Jordan coordinates with a neighbor to watch Mateo for an hour so they can drive Lucia to her appointment without dragging him along.

Extended family responsibilities also shape Jordan’s routine. Their mother, Carmen, lives alone in Milwaukee on a fixed income after decades working as a home health aide. In early December, her furnace failed during a cold snap, and while a local program helped cover part of the cost, Jordan chipped in a few hundred dollars from the money they’d set aside for January’s rent. They make a point of calling Carmen every Sunday evening, checking on her arthritis, reminding her about her medications, and talking about the kids. Jordan’s siblings, Daniel and Elena, help when they can, but Jordan is the one their mom calls first when something breaks or a bill looks confusing. A typical weekend for Jordan involves a video call with family, grocery runs timed around sales, meal prepping simple dishes that stretch—big pots of chili, rice and beans, casseroles—and squeezing in time to grade papers at the kitchen table after the kids are in bed.

Despite the financial stress, there’s a sense of stubborn optimism in Jordan’s outlook. They’ve cut back on non-essential spending—dropping streaming services, sticking to a strict grocery list, and watching every utility bill for usage spikes. They’ve already worked out payment arrangements with their landlord and the utility company, and they keep a meticulous spreadsheet on their laptop tracking due dates, minimum payments, and paychecks. When they talk about the future with Renee, it’s with a mix of realism and hope: if they can get fully current on rent and utilities and knock down the highest-interest debts, Jordan believes their combined income will be enough to maintain stability. In the meantime, they focus on what they can control—showing up for their students, being present with their kids, and carving out small moments of joy, like Saturday morning pancakes or reading fantasy novels after everyone else is asleep. Their life is full, sometimes overwhelming, but grounded in a quiet determination to keep their family safe, housed, and moving forward.

secrets.detail_content: Since February 2023, most of our savings have gone toward paying off the $4,800 in medical bills from when my son Mateo was hospitalized for three nights at UW Health American Family Children’s Hospital in Madison for a severe asthma attack after he caught COVID at Eastside Middle School, which has left us unable to catch up on past-due rent and utility payments during the ongoing pandemic.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a child’s specific medical condition, hospitalization details, and the exact amount of medical debt, as well as implying a possible link to the applicant’s workplace. While relevant to financial hardship, it overshares protected health information about a minor and precise financial details that are not necessary for a loan committee to know at this level of specificity.

places: UW Health American Family Children’s Hospital

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Mateo’s hospital bills

Mami, UW Health just sent the final statement — it’s $4,800 for the three nights Mateo was at American Family after he caught COVID and had that awful asthma attack in February 2023. We’ve basically wiped out our savings trying to keep up.
Pandemic impact notes: Feb 2023 – Mateo’s 3-night stay at American Family for COVID/asthma → $4,800 bill spread over 18 months, drained emergency fund.

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: After my partner Renee was laid off for six weeks from Lakeside Grill during the indoor dining shutdown in April–May 2020, we fell two months behind on rent at our apartment on Maple Crest Drive, and our landlord at Maple Crest Apartments filed a 5-day eviction notice in Dane County Court, which is still showing up on our rental record and making it harder to move somewhere more affordable even though we eventually paid the full $2,100 balance.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a specific past eviction proceeding, exact rent arrears, and an ongoing legal/records issue with housing, which could expose the applicant to stigma or bias and is more detailed than needed to demonstrate pandemic-related hardship.

appears_naturally_in.example: We’re still feeling the fallout from when Renee got laid off from Lakeside in April–May 2020. We ended up 2 months behind on rent at Maple Crest and got a 5-day eviction notice in Dane County Court. Even though we paid the $2,100, it’s still on our record and making it hard to find a cheaper place.

Subject: Question about eviction record from 2020

In spring 2020, after my partner was out of work for six weeks due to the dining shutdown, we fell behind on two months’ rent (about $2,100) at Maple Crest Apartments on Maple Crest Drive and received a 5-day eviction notice filed in Dane County Court. We paid it in full but it still appears on our rental history. Is there any way to get this sealed or removed?

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: Because my daughter Lucia’s preschool flagged concerns about her developmental delays during her screening at the Goodman Community Center in September 2022, we’ve been taking her twice a month to the UW Health Child Development Clinic, and the $65 co-pays plus gas from our place on Maple Crest Drive to the clinic have added about $180 a month in extra expenses since last fall that we just can’t absorb with Renee’s reduced hours.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares a young child’s specific developmental concerns, the clinic name, and detailed cost information. It exposes sensitive pediatric health and developmental information about a family member that is not strictly necessary for assessing general financial hardship.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Financial strain from Lucia’s appointments

Since Lucia’s screening at Goodman in Sept 2022, we’ve been going to the UW Health Child Development Clinic twice a month. The $65 co-pays plus gas from Maple Crest are adding about $180/month, which is really hard with Renee’s hours still cut back.

Event: Lucia – UW Child Development Clinic
Location: UW Health Child Development Clinic
Notes: 2nd September 2023 visit – $65 co-pay, leave Maple Crest by 2:15 pm.

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: In January 2021 I started seeing therapist Dr. Angela Ruiz at Journey Mental Health Center on East Washington after I had a panic attack during a virtual IEP meeting for my 7th graders, and the $40 weekly co-pay plus needing to cut back my hours on the after-school program at Eastside Middle School have reduced our income by about $260 a month while we’re still dealing with pandemic-related stress and bills.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses the applicant’s mental health treatment, provider name, clinic, and a specific panic attack at work, which could invite stigma or bias from reviewers and goes beyond what is necessary to explain financial hardship.

places: Journey Mental Health Center on East Washington
events: panic attack during virtual IEP meeting

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Finally starting therapy

I had a full-on panic attack during a virtual IEP meeting with my 7th graders last week, so I finally made an appointment with Dr. Angela Ruiz at Journey Mental Health on East Wash. It’s $40/week, and I had to drop some after-school hours, so we’re losing about $260/month, but I really need the support.

Event: Session w/ Dr. Ruiz
Location: Journey Mental Health – E. Washington
Notes: Weekly therapy (started Jan 2021 after IEP panic attack).

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: When my mom Carmen caught COVID in November 2020 and was hospitalized for five days at Aurora St. Luke’s in Milwaukee, I drove from Madison to Milwaukee three weekends in a row to help her and my sister Elena, and put about $900 of gas, parking, and groceries for their apartment on my Chase Freedom credit card, which I’m still paying off at 24% interest on top of our own pandemic-related bills.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a parent’s specific COVID illness and hospitalization, as well as precise financial and credit card details, which implicate another adult’s medical privacy and expose the applicant’s vulnerable debt situation more than is needed for this form.

places: Aurora St. Luke’s Medical Center in Milwaukee

appears_naturally_in.example: I’ve driven down to Milwaukee three weekends in a row since Mami went into Aurora St. Luke’s with COVID in November. I’ve put about $900 of gas, parking, and groceries on my Chase card at like 24% interest. Between that and our own pandemic bills, I’m drowning a bit.

Subject: Credit card debt from family medical emergency

In Nov 2020 my mom was hospitalized with COVID at Aurora St. Luke’s in Milwaukee for 5 days. I drove from Madison to Milwaukee three weekends in a row to help, and I charged about $900 in related expenses to my Chase Freedom card, which is at 24% interest. I’m still paying this off on top of our ongoing pandemic-related shortfalls.

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: In February 2023, when Renee tested positive for COVID a second time after a double shift at Lakeside Grill, we fell behind on our We Energies and MG&E bills, and I had to call St. Mary’s Hospital in Madison to set up a $35/month payment plan on the remaining $780 from her ER visit because we couldn’t afford to pay the full amount without skipping rent.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses Renee’s specific COVID status, a particular ER visit, the hospital name, and an exact unpaid medical balance and payment plan. The committee only needs to know there are pandemic-related medical bills and financial strain, not detailed health information about a partner or the exact debt structure, which could feel intrusive and invite judgment.

names: partner Renee Thompson (line cook at Lakeside Grill)

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Why I’m freaking out about money again

Elena, when Renee tested positive again in February 2023 after that double at Lakeside, we ended up with a $780 ER bill at St. Mary’s and I had to beg them for a $35/month plan so we wouldn’t lose the apartment over it.
Budget notes – March 2023: Don’t forget St. Mary’s $35 payment (Renee’s Feb 2023 COVID ER bill – $780 total).

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: After Mateo had COVID in November 2022, his asthma got worse, and in Spring 2023 his pediatric pulmonologist, Dr. Patel at UW Health East Clinic, increased his inhaler dose and added a second controller medication, which bumped our monthly out-of-pocket cost for his prescriptions from about $45 to around $160 even with insurance, and we ended up putting two of those months on a Capital One card that’s already near its limit.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares a child’s specific medical provider, clinic, treatment changes, and detailed prescription costs, plus the exact credit card used and its status. The committee only needs to know that a child’s health needs increased costs, not the doctor’s name, clinic, and precise medication/credit details, which exposes both health privacy and financial vulnerability.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Mateo’s lungs after COVID

Mami, after Mateo had COVID in November 2022, Dr. Patel at UW Health East upped his inhaler and added another controller in Spring 2023. Our share jumped from about $45 to $160 a month, so we had to put two months on the Capital One card.

Event: Follow-up with Dr. Patel – Mateo’s asthma meds review
Location: UW Health East Clinic

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: Because of the sudden school closures in January and March 2021 at Eastside Middle School, I used up all my sick and personal days staying home with Lucia and Mateo, and then had to take 3 unpaid days in March and 2 in April; my principal, Ms. Henderson, informally warned me in an email on April 19, 2021 that my attendance was ‘raising concerns’ even though the absences were for COVID-related childcare, which has made me afraid to call in sick now even when I probably should.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals an internal workplace issue: an informal warning from a named principal about attendance, which could be seen as a performance concern. The committee doesn’t need specific disciplinary or HR-related details to assess need, and including a supervisor’s name and quoted language could affect how reviewers perceive the applicant professionally.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Work is not getting it

Danny, after the January and March 2021 closures at Eastside, I burned all my leave and ended up with 3 unpaid days in March and 2 in April. Then Ms. Henderson emailed me on April 19 saying my attendance was ‘raising concerns.’ It’s all COVID childcare, but now I’m scared to take a sick day.
Draft to union rep: In March–April 2021 I took 5 unpaid days due to COVID school closures; on 4/19/21 Ms. Henderson sent an email referencing ‘attendance concerns.’

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: When our SNAP benefits were cut in June 2023, I started putting groceries from Woodman’s and Walmart on a Discover card, and by September 2023 we had about $1,950 on that card just from food and diapers; I even skipped my July and August 2023 student loan payments to keep up, which has now put those loans into early delinquency status according to the notice I got from FedLoan Servicing on September 28, 2023.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes beyond demonstrating need and discloses exact debt amounts, specific creditors, skipped student loan payments, and loan delinquency status, which could create stigma or perceived financial irresponsibility. The reviewers only need a general sense of hardship, not detailed credit and delinquency history.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: We need to talk about the Discover card

Renee, after the SNAP cut in June 2023, we’ve been putting Woodman’s and Walmart groceries on the Discover, and it’s up to about $1,950 now. I also skipped my July and August student loan payments and got an early delinquency notice from FedLoan on 9/28/23.
To-do: Call FedLoan re: early delinquency (missed July/Aug 2023 payments due to SNAP cut and Discover grocery charges).

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: In October 2022, after Renee’s second COVID infection and a stretch where she missed almost three weeks of work at Lakeside Grill, we got a 5-day notice from our property manager at Maple Crest Apartments taped to our door for being $742 behind on rent, and I had to borrow $400 from my mom Carmen in Milwaukee and agree to a written ‘last chance’ payment plan with the leasing office on October 17, 2022 to avoid an eviction filing.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares a specific 5-day notice, exact rent arrears, a ‘last chance’ payment plan, and the near-eviction situation, which is more detail than needed and could carry stigma or perceived risk as a borrower. The committee needs to know housing is unstable, but not the precise legal posture of the tenancy or the internal apartment management actions.

events: ‘last chance’ payment plan to avoid eviction filing

appears_naturally_in.example: They put a 5-day notice on our door at Maple Crest today. We’re $742 behind because Renee missed almost 3 weeks at Lakeside after COVID again. Mom lent me $400 and I signed a ‘last chance’ plan with the office on 10/17.

Subject: Thank you for helping with rent

Mami, that $400 you sent in October 2022 is what let us sign the payment plan with Maple Crest on 10/17/22 and stop the 5-day notice from turning into an eviction.

secrets.related_question: Coronavirus Pandemic Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: In December 2024, I had to miss three unpaid days at Eastside Middle School after my therapist, Dr. Lena Ortiz, at UW Health Behavioral Health Clinic strongly recommended I take emergency time off due to a severe depressive episode and panic attacks related to managing my 6th–8th grade caseload during hybrid instruction, which left us short on income and behind on our December rent and utilities.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses detailed mental health information (diagnosis-level detail, therapist name, specific clinic, and work impact) that could invite stigma or bias about Jordan’s reliability as an employee. The committee only needs to know there was a loss of income or health-related disruption, not the specifics of their mental health treatment or provider identity.

places: Eastside Middle School – Special Education Department
UW Health Behavioral Health Clinic, Madison

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Why my paycheck is short this month

Renee, Dr. Ortiz insisted I take those three unpaid days in December when the panic attacks got bad, so my check is lighter and that’s why we’re short on rent and Alliant this month.
To discuss with Dr. Ortiz on 12/19: guilt about missing 3 unpaid days at Eastside and how it’s put us behind on rent/utilities.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: On January 8, 2025, our son Mateo had a severe asthma flare-up at his 4th-grade school, Chavez Elementary, and we had to take him to the UW Health East Madison Hospital ER; the $450 ER copay and two follow-up visits wiped out the money we had set aside to catch up on November’s brake and tire credit card payment and January rent, so we’re now juggling late fees on both.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares a minor child’s specific medical condition and acute emergency treatment details, which are highly sensitive health information about a third party who is not the applicant. The committee only needs to understand unexpected medical expenses occurred, not the child’s diagnosis, school, or hospital details.

appears_naturally_in.example: We had to take Mateo to the UW East Madison ER on 1/8 after an asthma flare at Chavez. The $450 copay plus follow-ups is why we’re totally tapped out for rent.

I just wanted to follow up about Mateo’s asthma episode on 1/8. We ended up at UW Health East Madison ER that evening and have two follow-up appointments scheduled, which has been a big unexpected expense for us.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: In late November 2024, my younger sister, Elena, called from Milwaukee because she was short on tuition and facing withdrawal from her nursing program at MATC; I sent her $300 from our December budget to keep her enrolled, which, combined with the high-interest payment on the 2011 Honda CR-V brakes and tires, is what pushed us into falling a month behind on rent and winter utilities.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a sibling’s financial hardship and risk of academic withdrawal, which is private to the sister and not necessary for evaluating Jordan’s need. It also reveals specific transfer amounts and family financial dynamics that could be judged negatively (e.g., helping family instead of paying bills).

events: threat of withdrawal from nursing program

appears_naturally_in.example: I sent Elena $300 in late Nov so she wouldn’t get dropped from MATC, but between that and the CR-V brakes/tire payment, we’re now a month behind on rent + MG&E.
Mami, I helped Elena with $300 for her nursing tuition at MATC in November so she wouldn’t have to stop school. It means I’m short for December rent and utilities, but I’ll figure something out.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: Because my partner Renee’s hours at Lakeside Grill were cut again in early January 2025 after a COVID exposure shut down their evening shifts for 10 days, we had to put $220 of groceries and $95 of Lucia’s preschool fees at Little Sprouts Learning Center on a high-interest store card, which, on top of the car repair balance, has made it impossible to catch up on the missed rent payment.
secrets.why_sensitive: This includes specific employment and scheduling problems at the partner’s workplace and a child’s preschool, along with exact amounts and use of high-interest credit. It exposes the partner’s precarious work situation and the family’s detailed spending patterns, which are more than the committee needs to assess general financial hardship.

events: charges on high-interest store credit card

appears_naturally_in.example: When Lakeside shut down Renee’s evening shifts for 10 days after that COVID exposure in early Jan, we had to put $220 in groceries + $95 for Lucia’s preschool on the store card. With the car repair balance, catching up on rent feels impossible.
Budget note 1/15: Renee’s hours cut (10 days no evening shifts at Lakeside), $220 groceries + $95 Little Sprouts on card; rent still 1 month behind.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: In October 2024, I received an informal written warning from my department chair at Eastside Middle School after I missed two IEP meetings when I had to stay home with Lucia during a COVID outbreak at her preschool; I lost a half-day’s pay each time, and I’ve been afraid to ask for any additional time off since, which has made it harder to manage Mateo’s asthma appointments and has contributed to late fees and missed bill payments.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals internal employment issues (an informal written warning, missed IEP meetings, and lost pay) that could lead to negative judgments about Jordan’s professionalism and job security. It also exposes childcare and medical coordination struggles beyond what is needed to justify financial need, and could influence how the committee perceives Jordan as an employee in the same district.

names: department chair at Eastside Middle School (unnamed)
places: Eastside Middle School – Special Education Department
events: late fees and missed bill payments

appears_naturally_in.example: I got an informal written warning in October for missing two IEP meetings when Lucia’s preschool had a COVID outbreak and I had to stay home with her. I lost a half day’s pay each time, and now I’m scared to ask for more time off even for Mateo’s asthma appointments, which is part of why I’m racking up late fees.
Talking points for MTI rep: Oct 2024 informal warning for missed IEPs due to Lucia’s COVID preschool closure; lost 2 half days’ pay, now behind on bills and hesitant to request any more leave.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 1

secrets.detail_content: In late November 2024, after my principal, Dr. Karen Lewis, informally warned me that my frequent late arrivals due to my son Mateo’s asthma flare-ups at UW Health East Clinic were becoming a “pattern of concern,” I used a high‑interest CashNet payday advance of $600 to cover a missed November rent payment at Ridgeview Apartments and unexpected inhaler refill copays, which has snowballed into overdraft fees and collection calls that this loan would help me resolve.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a workplace performance concern, a specific administrator’s informal warning, a child’s detailed medical condition and treatment location, and the use of a payday lender, all of which could invite stigma, bias, or employment repercussions and are more detailed than needed to justify financial hardship on a union loan form.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Work stress + money mess

Elena, after Dr. Lewis pulled me aside right before Thanksgiving about being late again because of Mateo’s asthma appointments at UW Health East, I panicked and took out a $600 CashNet advance just to keep us from getting evicted from Ridgeview and to cover his inhaler copays. Now the overdraft fees and calls are killing us.

Budget note 11/30/24: CashNet $600 advance to cover missed November rent at Ridgeview + Mateo’s asthma meds after Dr. Lewis’s warning about tardiness. Need plan to pay off before collections escalate.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: In January 2025, my partner Renee’s hours at Lakeside Grill were cut again after a confrontation with her kitchen manager, Mark, about unsafe mask practices, and she was written up and temporarily taken off the schedule for 10 days, which wiped out the $450 we were counting on to pay our Alliant Energy bill and left us choosing between keeping the heat on in our Maple Crest Drive apartment and making the minimum payment on a past‑due CapitalOne card.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals another adult’s workplace conflict and disciplinary action, names a specific manager and employer, and discloses detailed household financial vulnerability and specific creditor names, which could lead to stigma or unintended sharing of Renee’s private employment issues that she has not consented to disclose to a union loan committee.

appears_naturally_in.example: Renee just got written up at Lakeside after arguing with her manager Mark about no one masking in the kitchen. They pulled her off the schedule for 10 days in January, so the $450 we were going to use for Alliant is gone and now we’re deciding between heat and the CapitalOne minimum.

Subject: Why I’ve been so distracted

Renee’s manager at Lakeside Grill, Mark, wrote her up for pushing back on the no-mask culture in the kitchen and cut her January shifts for 10 days. That $450 was supposed to cover our Alliant bill, so now we’re juggling that and a past‑due CapitalOne payment.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: On December 5, 2024, my mom, Carmen, slipped on the ice outside her apartment in Milwaukee and spent two nights at Froedtert Hospital for a fractured wrist; because she’s on a fixed income and her Medicare Advantage plan left her with a $780 out‑of‑pocket bill, I put the charges and my Greyhound trips back and forth from Madison on my already maxed‑out Chase card, which is now in a hardship program with a 60‑day past‑due status that I’m trying to keep from going to collections.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a parent’s specific medical incident, treatment location, insurance status, and financial struggles, as well as detailed creditor information and delinquency status, which goes beyond what is necessary to demonstrate need and shares another person’s health and financial information without their consent.

places: Greyhound station between Madison and Milwaukee
events: enrollment in credit card hardship program

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Mom’s hospital bill

Elena, after Mom fell on the ice on Dec 5 and ended up at Froedtert for two nights with that fractured wrist, her Medicare Advantage still left her with $780 to pay. I put it, plus my Greyhound trips from Madison, on my Chase card, which is now 60 days past due and in a hardship program.
Dec 5–7, 2024: Greyhound to Milwaukee – Mom at Froedtert (wrist fracture) – pay $780 OOP on Chase.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: Since September 2024, our daughter Lucia has been going to weekly occupational therapy at GHC‑South Clinic for mild developmental delays, and even with insurance we’re paying a $35 copay every Thursday plus about $18 in gas and parking each trip; when our 2010 Honda Civic needed a $930 brake and tire repair at Wilde East Towne Honda on October 14, I skipped two of Lucia’s sessions and then put the remaining visits on a CareCredit medical card that’s now at $1,420 and accruing interest we can’t realistically pay down without help.
secrets.why_sensitive: This gives detailed information about a young child’s medical/developmental status, treatment location, and schedule, and exposes specific debt instruments and balances, which is more granular health and financial data than needed for a loan committee and could affect how reviewers perceive the family.

events: skipped two therapy sessions due to cost

appears_naturally_in.example: Therapy budget: Lucia – GHC‑South, $35 copay + ~$18 gas/parking every Thurs since Sept. Had to skip 2 sessions after $930 brake/tire repair at Wilde East Towne Honda on 10/14; remaining visits on CareCredit (now ~$1,420).
If we keep taking Lucia to GHC‑South every Thurs ($35 + gas) after the $930 Honda repair, the CareCredit balance will just keep climbing. We’re already at about $1,420.

secrets.related_question: Other Reason For Request Extenuating Circumstances Description Line 2

secrets.detail_content: In early February 2025, I met with our school social worker, Megan Ortiz, in her office at Eastside Middle School to set up emergency grocery and gas assistance through the district’s family hardship fund after our checking account at UW Credit Union was hit with $216 in overdraft fees from automatic payments to Spectrum and T‑Mobile, which left us short on food and forced me to borrow $200 in cash from a coworker, Ms. Patel, that I still haven’t been able to pay back.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals the use of internal hardship resources at their workplace, specific coworkers’ names and roles, and detailed banking and overdraft information, creating potential embarrassment and stigma at work and sharing more precise financial distress than necessary for this form.

places: Eastside Middle School social work office
events: meeting to request family hardship fund support

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Thank you for meeting with me

Megan, thank you again for taking the time last week to help me access the family hardship fund. After the $216 in overdraft fees at UW Credit Union from the Spectrum and T‑Mobile autopays, we literally didn’t have enough left for groceries, and I had to borrow $200 from Ms. Patel.

Mom, I talked to the social worker at Eastside, Ms. Ortiz, and she got us some grocery cards from the hardship fund. Our UW Credit Union account got slammed with $216 in overdraft fees after Spectrum and T‑Mobile hit, and I even had to borrow $200 from a coworker.
